Remove this reference to each.value in your configuration to work around this error.`,\n208: \t\t\t\tSubject:  rng.ToHCL().Ptr(),\n209: \t\t\t})\n210: \t\t\treturn cty.UnknownVal(cty.DynamicPseudoType), diags\n211: \t\t}\n212: \tdefault:\n213: \t\tdiags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{\n214: \t\t\tSeverity: hcl.DiagError,\n215: \t\t\tSummary:  `Invalid \"each\" attribute`,\n216: \t\t\tDetail:   fmt.Sprintf(`The \"each\" object does not have an attribute named %q. The supported attributes are each.key and each.value, the current key and value pair of the \"for_each\" attribute set.`, addr.Name),\n217: \t\t\tSubject:  rng.ToHCL().Ptr(),\n218: \t\t})\n219: \t\treturn cty.DynamicVal, diags\n220: \t}\n221: \n222: \tif returnVal == cty.NilVal {\n223: \t\tdiags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{\n224: \t\t\tSeverity: hcl.DiagError,\n225: \t\t\tSummary:  `Reference to \"each\" in context without for_each`,\n226: \t\t\tDetail:   `The \"each\" object can be used only in \"module\" or \"resource\" blocks, and only when the \"for_each\" argument is set.`,\n227: \t\t\tSubject:  rng.ToHCL().Ptr(),\n228: \t\t})\n229: \t\treturn cty.UnknownVal(cty.DynamicPseudoType), diags\n230: \t}\n231: \treturn returnVal, diags\n232: }\n233: \n234: func (d *evaluationStateData) GetInputVariable(addr addrs.InputVariable, rng tfdiags.SourceRange) (cty.Value, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {\n235: \tvar diags tfdiags.Diagnostics\n236: \n237: \t// First we'll make sure the requested value is declared in configuration,\n238: \t// so we can produce a nice message if not.\n239: \tmoduleConfig := d.Evaluator.Config.DescendantForInstance(d.ModulePath)\n240: \tif moduleConfig == nil {\n241: \t\t// should never happen, since we can't be evaluating in a module\n242: \t\t// that wasn't mentioned in configuration.\n243: \t\tpanic(fmt.Sprintf(\"input variable read from %s, which has no configuration\", d.ModulePath))\n244: \t}\n245: \n246: \tconfig := moduleConfig.Module.Variables[addr.Name]\n247: \tif config == nil {\n248: \t\tvar suggestions []string\n249: \t\tfor k := range moduleConfig.Module.Variables {\n250: \t\t\tsuggestions = append(suggestions, k)\n251: \t\t}\n252: \t\tsuggestion := didyoumean.NameSuggestion(addr.Name, suggestions)\n253: \t\tif suggestion != \"\" {\n254: \t\t\tsuggestion = fmt.Sprintf(\" Did you mean %q?\", suggestion)\n255: \t\t} else {\n256: \t\t\tsuggestion = fmt.Sprintf(\" This variable can be declared with a variable %q {} block.\", addr.Name)\n257: \t\t}\n258: \n259: \t\tdiags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{\n260: \t\t\tSeverity: hcl.DiagError,\n261: \t\t\tSummary:  `Reference to undeclared input variable`,\n262: \t\t\tDetail:   fmt.Sprintf(`An input variable with the name %q has not been declared.%s`, addr.Name, suggestion),\n263: \t\t\tSubject:  rng.ToHCL().Ptr(),\n264: \t\t})\n265: \t\treturn cty.DynamicVal, diags\n266: \t}\n267: \n268: \t// During the validate walk, input variables are always unknown so\n269: \t// that we are validating the configuration for all possible input values\n270: \t// rather than for a specific set. Checking against a specific set of\n271: \t// input values then happens during the plan walk.\n272: \t//\n273: \t// This is important because otherwise the validation walk will tend to be\n274: \t// overly strict, requiring expressions throughout the configuration to\n275: \t// be complicated to accommodate all possible inputs, whereas returning\n276: \t// unknown here allows for simpler patterns like using input values as\n277: \t// guards to broadly enable/disable resources, avoid processing things\n278: \t// that are disabled, etc. Terraform's static validation leans towards\n279: \t// being liberal in what it accepts because the subsequent plan walk has\n280: \t// more information available and so can be more conservative.\n281: \tif d.Operation == walkValidate {\n282: \t\t// We should still capture the statically-configured marks during\n283: \t\t// the validate walk.\n284: \t\tret := cty.UnknownVal(config.Type)\n285: \t\tif config.Sensitive {\n286: \t\t\tret = ret.Mark(marks.Sensitive)\n287: \t\t}\n288: \t\tif config.Ephemeral {\n289: \t\t\tret = ret.Mark(marks.Ephemeral)\n290: \t\t}\n291: \t\treturn ret, diags\n292: \t}\n293: \n294: \tval := d.Evaluator.NamedValues.GetInputVariableValue(d.ModulePath.InputVariable(addr.Name))\n295: \n296: \t// Mark if sensitive and/or ephemeral\n297: \tif config.Sensitive {\n298: \t\tval = val.Mark(marks.Sensitive)\n299: \t}\n300: \tif config.Ephemeral {\n301: \t\tval = val.Mark(marks.Ephemeral)\n302: \t}\n303: \n304: \treturn val, diags\n305: }\n306: \n307: func (d *evaluationStateData) GetLocalValue(addr addrs.LocalValue, rng tfdiags.SourceRange) (cty.Value, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {\n308: \tvar diags tfdiags.Diagnostics\n309: \n310: \t// First we'll make sure the requested value is declared in configuration,\n311: \t// so we can produce a nice message if not.\n312: \tmoduleConfig := d.Evaluator.Config.DescendantForInstance(d.ModulePath)\n313: \tif moduleConfig == nil {\n314: \t\t// should never happen, since we can't be evaluating in a module\n315: \t\t// that wasn't mentioned in configuration.\n316: \t\tpanic(fmt.Sprintf(\"local value read from %s, which has no configuration\", d.ModulePath))\n317: \t}\n318: \n319: \tconfig := moduleConfig.Module.Locals[addr.Name]\n320: \tif config == nil {\n321: \t\tvar suggestions []string\n322: \t\tfor k := range moduleConfig.Module.Locals {\n323: \t\t\tsuggestions = append(suggestions, k)\n324: \t\t}\n325: \t\tsuggestion := didyoumean.NameSuggestion(addr.Name, suggestions)\n326: \t\tif suggestion != \"\" {\n327: \t\t\tsuggestion = fmt.Sprintf(\" Did you mean %q?\", suggestion)\n328: \t\t}\n329: \n330: \t\tdiags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{\n331: \t\t\tSeverity: hcl.DiagError,\n332: \t\t\tSummary:  `Reference to undeclared local value`,\n333: \t\t\tDetail:   fmt.Sprintf(`A local value with the name %q has not been declared.%s`, addr.Name, suggestion),\n334: \t\t\tSubject:  rng.ToHCL().Ptr(),\n335: \t\t})\n336: \t\treturn cty.DynamicVal, diags\n337: \t}\n338: \n339: \tval := d.Evaluator.NamedValues.GetLocalValue(addr.Absolute(d.ModulePath))\n340: \treturn val, diags\n341: }\n342: \n343: func (d *evaluationStateData) GetModule(addr addrs.ModuleCall, rng tfdiags.SourceRange) (cty.Value, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {\n344: \tvar diags tfdiags.Diagnostics\n345: \n346: \t// Output results live in the module that declares them, which is one of\n347: \t// the child module instances of our current module path.\n348: \tmoduleAddr := d.ModulePath.Module().Child(addr.Name)\n349: \tabsAddr := addr.Absolute(d.ModulePath)\n350: \n351: \tparentCfg := d.Evaluator.Config.DescendantForInstance(d.ModulePath)\n352: \tcallConfig, ok := parentCfg.Module.ModuleCalls[addr.Name]\n353: \tif !ok {\n354: \t\tdiags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{\n355: \t\t\tSeverity: hcl.DiagError,\n356: \t\t\tSummary:  `Reference to undeclared module`,\n357: \t\t\tDetail:   fmt.Sprintf(`The configuration contains no %s.`, moduleAddr),\n358: \t\t\tSubject:  rng.ToHCL().Ptr(),\n359: \t\t})\n360: \t\treturn cty.DynamicVal, diags\n361: \t}\n362: \n363: \t// We'll consult the configuration to see what output names we are\n364: \t// expecting, so we can ensure the resulting object is of the expected\n365: \t// type even if our data is incomplete for some reason.\n366: \tmoduleConfig := d.Evaluator.Config.Descendant(moduleAddr)\n367: \tif moduleConfig == nil {\n368: \t\t// should never happen, since we have a valid module call above, this\n369: \t\t// should be caught during static validation.\n370: \t\tpanic(fmt.Sprintf(\"output value read from %s, which has no configuration\", moduleAddr))\n371: \t}\n372: \toutputConfigs := moduleConfig.Module.Outputs\n373: \n374: \t// We don't do instance expansion during validation, and so we need to\n375: \t// return an unknown value. Technically we should always return\n376: \t// cty.DynamicVal here because the final value during plan will always\n377: \t// be an object or tuple type with unpredictable attributes/elements,\n378: \t// but because we never actually carry values forward from validation to\n379: \t// planning we lie a little here and return unknown list and map types,\n380: \t// just to give us more opportunities to catch author mistakes during\n381: \t// validation.\n382: \t//\n383: \t// This means that in practice any expression that refers to a module\n384: \t// call must be written to be valid for either a collection type or\n385: \t// structural type of similar kind, so that it can be considered as\n386: \t// valid during both the validate and plan walks.\n387: \tif d.Operation == walkValidate {\n388: \t\tatys := make(map[string]cty.Type, len(outputConfigs))\n389: \t\tfor name := range outputConfigs {\n390: \t\t\tatys[name] = cty.DynamicPseudoType // output values are dynamically-typed\n391: \t\t}\n392: \t\tinstTy := cty.Object(atys)\n393: \n394: \t\tswitch {\n395: \t\tcase callConfig.Count != nil:\n396: \t\t\treturn cty.UnknownVal(cty.List(instTy)), diags\n397: \t\tcase callConfig.ForEach != nil:\n398: \t\t\treturn cty.UnknownVal(cty.Map(instTy)), diags\n399: \t\tdefault:\n400: \t\t\treturn cty.UnknownVal(instTy), diags\n401: \t\t}\n402: \t}\n403: \n404: \t// For all other walk types, we proceed to dynamic evaluation of individual\n405: \t// instances, using the global instance expander. An earlier graph node\n406: \t// should always have registered the expansion of this module call before\n407: \t// we get here, unless there's a bug in the graph builders.\n408: \tallInstances := d.Evaluator.Instances\n409: \tinstKeyType, instKeys, known := allInstances.ExpandAbsModuleCall(absAddr)\n410: \tif !known {\n411: \t\t// If we don't know which instances exist then we can't really predict\n412: \t\t// anything at all. We can't even predict the return type based on\n413: \t\t// instKeyType because output values are dynamically-typed and so\n414: \t\t// our final result will always be an object or tuple type whose\n415: \t\t// attribute/element count we cannot predict.\n416: \t\treturn cty.DynamicVal, diags\n417: \t}\n418: \n419: \tinstanceObjVal := func(instKey addrs.InstanceKey) (cty.Value, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {\n420: \t\t// This function must always return a valid value, even if it's\n421: \t\t// just a cty.DynamicVal placeholder accompanying error diagnostics.\n422: \t\tvar diags tfdiags.Diagnostics\n423: \n424: \t\tnamedVals := d.Evaluator.NamedValues\n425: \t\tmoduleInstAddr := absAddr.Instance(instKey)\n426: \t\tattrs := make(map[string]cty.Value, len(outputConfigs))\n427: \t\tfor name := range outputConfigs {\n428: \t\t\toutputAddr := moduleInstAddr.OutputValue(name)\n429: \n430: \t\t\t// Although we do typically expect the graph dependencies to\n431: \t\t\t// ensure that values get registered before they are needed,\n432: \t\t\t// we track depedencies with specific output values where\n433: \t\t\t// possible, instead of with entire module calls, and so\n434: \t\t\t// in this specific case it's valid for some of this call's\n435: \t\t\t// output values to not be known yet, with the graph builder\n436: \t\t\t// being responsible for making sure that no expression\n437: \t\t\t// in the configuration can actually observe that.\n438: \t\t\tif !namedVals.HasOutputValue(outputAddr) {\n439: \t\t\t\tattrs[name] = cty.DynamicVal\n440: \t\t\t\tcontinue\n441: \t\t\t}\n442: \t\t\toutputVal := namedVals.GetOutputValue(outputAddr)\n443: \t\t\tattrs[name] = outputVal\n444: \t\t}\n445: \n446: \t\treturn cty.ObjectVal(attrs), diags\n447: \t}\n448: \n449: \tswitch instKeyType {\n450: \n451: \tcase addrs.NoKeyType:\n452: \t\t// In this case we should always have exactly one instance that\n453: \t\t// is addrs.NoKey. If not then there's a bug in the [instances.Expander]\n454: \t\t// implementation.\n455: \t\tif len(instKeys) != 1 {\n456: \t\t\tpanic(fmt.Sprintf(\"module call has no instance key type but has %d instances (should be 1)\", len(instKeys)))\n457: \t\t}\n458: \t\tret, moreDiags := instanceObjVal(instKeys[0])\n459: \t\tdiags = diags.Append(moreDiags)\n460: \t\treturn ret, diags\n461: \n462: \tcase addrs.IntKeyType:\n463: \t\t// We can assume that the instance keys are in ascending numerical order\n464: \t\t// and are consecutive, per the contract of allInstances.ExpandModuleCall.\n465: \t\telems := make([]cty.Value, 0, len(instKeys))\n466: \t\tfor _, instKey := range instKeys {\n467: \t\t\tinstVal, moreDiags := instanceObjVal(instKey)\n468: \t\t\telems = append(elems, instVal)\n469: \t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(moreDiags)\n470: \t\t}\n471: \t\treturn cty.TupleVal(elems), diags\n472: \n473: \tcase addrs.StringKeyType:\n474: \t\tattrs := make(map[string]cty.Value, len(instKeys))\n475: \t\tfor _, instKey := range instKeys {\n476: \t\t\tinstVal, moreDiags := instanceObjVal(instKey)\n477: \t\t\tattrs[string(instKey.(addrs.StringKey))] = instVal\n478: \t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(moreDiags)\n479: \t\t}\n480: \t\treturn cty.ObjectVal(attrs), diags\n481: \n482: \tdefault:\n483: \t\tdiags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{\n484: \t\t\tSeverity: hcl.DiagError,\n485: \t\t\tSummary:  `Unsupported instance key type`,\n486: \t\t\tDetail: fmt.Sprintf(\n487: \t\t\t\t`Module call %s has instance key type %#v, which is not supported by the expression evaluator. This is a bug in Terraform.`,\n488: \t\t\t\tabsAddr, instKeyType,\n489: \t\t\t),\n490: \t\t\tSubject: rng.ToHCL().Ptr(),\n491: \t\t})\n492: \t\treturn cty.DynamicVal, diags\n493: \t}\n494: }\n495: \n496: func (d *evaluationStateData) GetResource(addr addrs.Resource, rng tfdiags.SourceRange) (cty.Value, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {\n497: \tvar diags tfdiags.Diagnostics\n498: \t// First we'll consult configuration.\n505: \t\tpanic(fmt.Sprintf(\"resource value read from %s, which has no configuration\", moduleAddr))\n506: \t}\n507: \n508: \tconfig := moduleConfig.Module.ResourceByAddr(addr)\n509: \t}\n518: \n519: \t// Much of this function was written before we had factored out the handling\n520: \t// of instance keys into the separate instance expander model, and so it\n521: \t// does a bunch of instance-related work itself below.\n522: \t//\n523: \t// Currently, unknown instance keys are only possible when planning with\n524: \t// DeferralAllowed set to true in the PlanOpts, which should only be the\n525: \t// case in the stacks runtime (not the \"normal terraform\" modules runtime).\n526: \t// Thus, we have some amount of duplicated code remaining, to be more\n527: \t// certain that stacks-specific behaviors won't leak out into the standard\n528: \t// runtime.\n529: \t//\n530: \t// TODO: When deferred actions are more stable and robust in stacks, it\n531: \t// would be nice to rework this function to rely on the ResourceInstanceKeys\n532: \t// result for _all_ of its work, rather than continuing to duplicate a bunch\n533: \t// of the logic we've tried to encapsulate over ther already.\n534: \tif d.Operation == walkPlan || d.Operation == walkApply {\n535: \t\tif _, _, hasUnknownKeys := d.Evaluator.Instances.ResourceInstanceKeys(addr.Absolute(moduleAddr)); hasUnknownKeys {\n536: \t\t\t// There really isn't anything interesting we can do in this situation,\n537: \t\t\t// because it means we have an unknown for_each/count, in which case\n538: \t\t\t// we can't even predict what the result type will be because it\n539: \t\t\t// would be either an object or tuple type decided based on the instance\n540: \t\t\t// keys.\n541: \t\t\t// (We can't get in here for a single-instance resource because in that\n542: \t\t\t// case we would know that there's only one key and it's addrs.NoKey,\n543: \t\t\t// so we'll fall through to the other logic below.)\n544: \t\t\tunknownVal := cty.DynamicVal\n545: \n546: \t\t\t// If an ephemeral resource is deferred we need to mark the returned unknown value as ephemeral\n547: \t\t\tif addr.Mode == addrs.EphemeralResourceMode {\n548: \t\t\t\tunknownVal = unknownVal.Mark(marks.Ephemeral)\n549: \t\t\t}\n550: \t\t\treturn unknownVal, diags\n551: \t\t}\n552: \t}\n553: \n554: \t// Build the provider address from configuration, since we may not have\n555: \t// state available in all cases.\n556: \t// We need to build an abs provider address, but we can use a default\n557: \t// instance since we're only interested in the schema.\n558: \tschema := d.getResourceSchema(addr, config.Provider)\n559: \tif schema == nil {\n560: \t\t// This shouldn't happen, since validation before we get here should've\n561: \t\t// taken care of it, but we'll show a reasonable error message anyway.\n562: \t\tdiags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{\n563: \t\t\tSeverity: hcl.DiagError,\n564: \t\t\tSummary: This is a bug in Terraform and should be reported.\", addr, config.Provider),\n566: \t\t\tSubject:  rng.ToHCL().Ptr(),\n567: \t\t})\n568: \t\treturn cty.DynamicVal, diags\n569: \t}\n570: \tty := schema.ImpliedType()\n571: \n572: \tif addr.Mode == addrs.EphemeralResourceMode {\n573: \t\t// FIXME: This does not yet work with deferrals, and it would be nice to\n574: \t\t// find some way to refactor this so that the following code is not so\n575: \t\t// tethered to the current implementation details. Instead we should\n576: \t\t// have an abstract idea of first determining what instances the\n577: \t\t// resource has (using d.Evaluator.Instances.ResourceInstanceKeys) and\n578: \t\t// then retrieving the value for each instance to assemble into the\n579: \t\t// result, using some per-resource-mode logic maintained elsewhere.\n580: \t\treturn d.getEphemeralResource(addr, rng)\n581: \t}\n582: \n583: \trs := d.Evaluator.State.Resource(addr.Absolute(d.ModulePath))\n584: \n585: \tif rs == nil {\n586: \t\tswitch d.Operation {\n587: \t\tcase walkPlan, walkApply:\n588: \t\t\t// During plan and apply as we evaluate each removed instance they\n589: \t\t\t// are removed from the working state. Since we know there are no\n590: \t\t\t// instances, return an empty container of the expected type.\n591: \t\t\tswitch {\n592: \t\t\tcase config.Count != nil:\n593: \t\t\t\treturn cty.EmptyTupleVal, diags\n594: \t\t\tcase config.ForEach != nil:\n595: \t\t\t\treturn cty.EmptyObjectVal, diags\n596: \t\t\tdefault:\n597: \t\t\t\t// While we can reference an expanded resource with 0\n598: \t\t\t\t// instances, we cannot reference instances that do not exist.\n599: \t\t\t\t// Due to the fact that we may have direct references to\n600: \t\t\t\t// instances that may end up in a root output during destroy\n601: \t\t\t\t// (since a planned destroy cannot yet remove root outputs), we\n602: \t\t\t\t// need to return a dynamic value here to allow evaluation to\n603: \t\t\t\t// continue.\n604: \t\t\t\tlog.Printf(\"[ERROR] unknown instance %q referenced during %s\", addr.Absolute(d.ModulePath), d.Operation)\n605: \t\t\t\treturn cty.DynamicVal, diags\n606: \t\t\t}\n607: \n608: \t\tcase walkImport:\n609: \t\t\t// Import does not yet plan resource changes, so new resources from\n610: \t\t\t// config are not going to be found here. Once walkImport fully\n611: \t\t\t// plans resources, this case should not longer be needed.\n612: \t\t\t// In the single instance case, we can return a typed unknown value\n613: \t\t\t// for the instance to better satisfy other expressions using the\n614: \t\t\t// value. A deferred resource overrides anything that might be\n645: \t// in the state for the resource, so we do this first.\n646: \tfor key, value := range d.Evaluator.Deferrals.GetDeferredResourceInstances(addr.Absolute(d.ModulePath)) {\n647: \t\tinstances[key] = value\n648: \t}\n649: \n650: \t// Decode all instances in the current state\n651: \tpendingDestroy := d.Operation == walkDestroy\n652: \tfor key, is := range rs.Instances {\n653: \t\tif _, ok := instances[key]; ok {\n654: \t\t\t// Then we've already loaded this instance from the deferrals so\n655: \t\t\t// we'll just ignore it being in state.\n656: \t\t\tcontinue\n657: \t\t}\n658: \t\t// Otherwise, we'll load the instance from state.\n659: \n660: \t\tif is == nil || is.Current == nil {\n661: \t\t\t// Assume we're dealing with an instance that hasn't been created yet.\n662: \t\t\tinstances[key] = cty.UnknownVal(ty)\n663: \t\t\tcontinue\n664: \t\t}\n665: \n666: \t\tinstAddr := addr.Instance(key).Absolute(d.ModulePath)\n667: \t\tchange := d.Evaluator.Changes.GetResourceInstanceChange(instAddr, addrs.NotDeposed)\n668: \t\tif change != nil {\n669: \t\t\t// Don't take any resources that are yet to be deleted into account.\n670: \t\t\t// If the referenced resource is CreateBeforeDestroy, then orphaned\n671: \t\t\t// instances will be in the state, as they are not destroyed until\n672: \t\t\t// after their dependants are updated.\n673: \t\t\tif change.Action == plans.Delete {\n674: \t\t\t\tif !pendingDestroy {\n675: \t\t\t\t\tcontinue\n676: \t\t\t\t}\n677: \t\t\t}\n678: \t\t}\n679: \n680: \t\t// Planned resources are temporarily stored in state with empty values,\n681: \t\t// and need to be replaced by the planned value here.\n682: 4: package terraform
5: 
6: import (
7: 	"github.com/zclconf/go-cty/cty"
8: 
9: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/addrs"
10: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/plans"
11: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/providers"
12: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/states"
13: )
14: 
15: // HookAction is an enum of actions that can be taken as a result of a hook
16: // callback. This allows you to modify the behavior of Terraform at runtime.
17: type HookAction byte
18: 
19: const (
20: 	// HookActionContinue continues with processing as usual.
21: 	HookActionContinue HookAction = iota
22: 
23: 	// HookActionHalt halts immediately: no more hooks are processed
24: 	// and the action that Terraform was about to take is cancelled.
25: 	HookActionHalt
26: )
27: 
28: // HookResourceIdentity is passed to Hook interface methods to fully identify
29: // the resource instance being operated on. It currently includes the resource
30: // address and the provider address.
31: type HookResourceIdentity struct {
32: 	Addr         addrs.AbsResourceInstance
33: 	ProviderAddr addrs.Provider
34: }
35: 
36: // Hook is the interface that must be implemented to hook into various
37: // parts of Terraform, allowing you to inspect or change behavior at runtime.
38: //
39: // There are MANY hook points into Terraform. If you only want to implement
40: // some hook points, but not all (which is the likely case), then embed the
41: // NilHook into your struct, which implements all of the interface but does
42: // nothing. Then, override only the functions you want to implement.
43: type Hook interface {
44: 	// PreApply and PostApply are called before and after an action for a
45: 	// single instance is applied. The error argument in PostApply is the
46: 	// error, if any, that was returned from the provider Apply call itself.
47: 	PreApply(id HookResourceIdentity, dk addrs.DeposedKey, action plans.Action, priorState, plannedNewState cty.Value) (HookAction, error)
48: 	PostApply(id HookResourceIdentity, dk addrs.DeposedKey, newState cty.Value, err error) (HookAction, error)
49: 
50: 	// PreDiff and PostDiff are called before and after a provider is given
51: 	// the opportunity to customize the proposed new state to produce the
52: 	// planned new state.
53: 	PreDiff(id HookResourceIdentity, dk addrs.DeposedKey, priorState, proposedNewState cty.Value) (HookAction, error)
54: 	PostDiff(id HookResourceIdentity, dk addrs.DeposedKey, action plans.Action, priorState, plannedNewState cty.Value) (HookAction, error)
55: 
56: 	// The provisioning hooks signal both the overall start end end of
57: 	// provisioning for a particular instance and of each of the individual
58: 	// configured provisioners for each instance. The sequence of these
59: 	// for a given instance might look something like this:
60: 	//
61: 	//          PreProvisionInstance(aws_instance.foo[1], ...)
62: 	//      PreProvisionInstanceStep(aws_instance.foo[1], "file")
63: 	//     PostProvisionInstanceStep(aws_instance.foo[1], "file", nil)
64: 	//      PreProvisionInstanceStep(aws_instance.foo[1], "remote-exec")
65: 	//               ProvisionOutput(aws_instance.foo[1], "remote-exec", "Installing foo...")
66: 	//               ProvisionOutput(aws_instance.foo[1], "remote-exec", "Configuring bar...")
67: 	//     PostProvisionInstanceStep(aws_instance.foo[1], "remote-exec", nil)
68: 	//         PostProvisionInstance(aws_instance.foo[1], ...)
69: 	//
70: 	// ProvisionOutput is called with output sent back by the provisioners.
71: 	// This will be called multiple times as output comes in, with each call
72: 	// representing one line of output. It cannot control whether the
73: 	// provisioner continues running.
74: 	PreProvisionInstance(id HookResourceIdentity, state cty.Value) (HookAction, error)
75: 	PostProvisionInstance(id HookResourceIdentity, state cty.Value) (HookAction, error)
76: 	PreProvisionInstanceStep(id HookResourceIdentity, typeName string) (HookAction, error)
77: 	PostProvisionInstanceStep(id HookResourceIdentity, typeName string, err error) (HookAction, error)
78: 	ProvisionOutput(id HookResourceIdentity, typeName string, line string)
79: 
80: 	// PreRefresh and PostRefresh are called before and after a single
81: 	// resource state is refreshed, respectively.
82: 	PreRefresh(id HookResourceIdentity, dk addrs.DeposedKey, priorState cty.Value) (HookAction, error)
83: 	PostRefresh(id HookResourceIdentity, dk addrs.DeposedKey, priorState cty.Value, newState cty.Value) (HookAction, error)
84: 
85: 	// PreImportState and PostImportState are called before and after
86: 	// (respectively) each state import operation for a given resource address when
87: 	// using the legacy import command.
88: 	PreImportState(id HookResourceIdentity, importID string) (HookAction, error)
89: 	PostImportState(id HookResourceIdentity, imported []providers.ImportedResource) (HookAction, error)
90: 
91: 	// PrePlanImport and PostPlanImport are called during a plan before and after planning to import
92: 	// a new resource using the configuration-driven import workflow.
93: 	PrePlanImport(id HookResourceIdentity, importID string) (HookAction, error)
94: 	PostPlanImport(id HookResourceIdentity, imported []providers.ImportedResource) (HookAction, error)
95: 
96: 	// PreApplyImport and PostApplyImport are called during an apply for each imported resource when
97: 	// using the configuration-driven import workflow.
98: 	PreApplyImport(id HookResourceIdentity, importing plans.ImportingSrc) (HookAction, error)
99: 	PostApplyImport(id HookResourceIdentity, importing plans.ImportingSrc) (HookAction, error)
100: 
101: 	// PreEphemeralOp and PostEphemeralOp are called during an operation on ephemeral resource
102: 	// such as opening, renewal or closing
103: 	PreEphemeralOp(id HookResourceIdentity, action plans.Action) (HookAction, error)
104: 	PostEphemeralOp(id HookResourceIdentity, action plans.Action, opErr error) (HookAction, error)
105: 
106: 	// Stopping is called if an external signal requests that Terraform
107: 	// gracefully abort an operation in progress.
108: 	//
109: 	// This notification might suggest that the user wants Terraform to exit
110: 	// ASAP and in that case it's possible that if Terraform runs for too much
111: 	// longer then it'll get killed un-gracefully, and so this hook could be
112: 	// an opportunity to persist any transient data that would be lost under
113: 	// a subsequent kill signal. However, implementations must take care to do
114: 	// so in a way that won't cause corruption if the process _is_ killed while
115: 	// this hook is still running.
116: 	//
117: 	// This hook cannot control whether Terraform continues, because the
118: 	// graceful shutdown process is typically already running by the time this
119: 	// function is called.
120: 	Stopping()
121: 
122: 	// PostStateUpdate is called each time the state is updated. The caller must
123: 	// coordinate a lock for the state if necessary, such that the Hook may
124: 	// access it freely without any need for additional locks to protect from
125: 	// concurrent writes. Implementations which modify or retain the state after
126: 	// the call has returned must copy the state.
127: 	PostStateUpdate(new *states.State) (HookAction, error)
128: }
129: 
130: // NilHook is a Hook implementation that does nothing. It exists only to
131: // simplify implementing hooks. You can embed this into your Hook implementation
132: // and only implement the functions you are interested in.
133: type NilHook struct{}
134: 
135: var _ Hook = (*NilHook)(nil)

ct != 0 {\\n243: \\t\\t\\tpanic(fmt.Sprintf(\\\"check state says that %s should have no rules, but it has %d\\\", addr, ct))\\n244: \\t\\t}\\n245: \\t\\treturn diags\\n246: \\t}\\n247: \\n248: \\t// We'll build just one evaluation context covering the data needed by\\n249: \\t// all of the rules together, since that'll minimize lock contention\\n250: \\t// on the state, plan, etc.\\n251: \\tscope := ctx.EvaluationScope(nil, nil, EvalDataForNoInstanceKey)\\n252: \\tvar refs []*addrs.Reference\\n253: \\tfor _, rule := range rules {\\n254: \\t\\tcondRefs, moreDiags := langrefs.ReferencesInExpr(addrs.ParseRef, rule.Condition)\\n255: \\t\\tdiags = diags.Append(moreDiags)\\n256: \\t\\tmsgRefs, moreDiags := langrefs.ReferencesInExpr(addrs.ParseRef, rule.ErrorMessage)\\n257: \\t\\tdiags = diags.Append(moreDiags)\\n258: \\t\\trefs = append(refs, condRefs...)\\n259: \\t\\trefs = append(refs, msgRefs...)\\n260: \\t}\\n261: \\tif diags.HasErrors() {\\n262: \\t\\t// If any of the references were invalid then evaluating the expressions\\n263: \\t\\t// will duplicate those errors, so we'll bail out early.\\n264: \\t\\treturn diags\\n265: \\t}\\n266: \\thclCtx, moreDiags := scope.EvalContext(refs)\\n267: \\tdiags = diags.Append(moreDiags)\\n268: \\tif moreDiags.HasErrors() {\\n269: \\t\\treturn diags\\n270: \\t}\\n271: \\n272: \\t// HACK: Historically we manually built a very constrained hcl.EvalContext\\n273: \\t// here, which only included the value of the one specific input variable\\n274: \\t// we're validating, since we didn't yet support referring to anything\\n275: \\t// else. That accidentally bypassed our rule that input variables are\\n276: \\t// always unknown during the validate walk, and thus accidentally created\\n277: \\t// a useful behavior of actually checking constant-only values against\\n278: \\t// their validation rules just during \\\"terraform validate\\\", rather than\\n279: \\t// having to run \\\"terraform plan\\\".\\n280: \\t//\\n281: \\t// Although that behavior was accidental, it makes simple validation rules\\n282: \\t// more useful and is protected by compatibility promises, and so we'll\\n283: \\t// fake it here by overwriting the unknown value that scope.EvalContext\\n284: \\t// will have inserted with a possibly-more-known value using the same\\n285: \\t// strategy our special code used to use.\\n286: \\tourVal := ctx.NamedValues().GetInputVariableValue(addr)\\n287: \\tif ourVal != cty.NilVal {\\n288: \\t\\t// (it would be weird for ourVal to be nil here, but we'll tolerate it\\n289: \\t\\t// because it was scope.EvalContext's responsibility to check for the\\n290: \\t\\t// absent final value, and even if it didn't we'll just get an\\n291: \\t\\t// evaluation error when evaluating the expressions below anyway.)\\n292: \\n293: \\t\\t// Our goal here is to make sure that a reference to the variable\\n294: \\t\\t// we're checking will evaluate to ourVal, regardless of what else\\n295: \\t\\t// scope.EvalContext might have put in the variables table.\\n296: \\t\\tif hclCtx.Variables == nil {\\n297: \\t\\t\\thclCtx.Variables = make(map[string]cty.Value)\\n298: \\t\\t}\\n299: \\t\\tif varsVal, ok := hclCtx.Variables[\\\"var\\\"]; ok {\\n300: \\t\\t\\t// Unfortunately we need to unpack and repack the object here,\\n301: \\t\\t\\t// because cty values are immutable.\\n302: \\t\\t\\tattrs := varsVal.AsValueMap()\\n303: \\t\\t\\tattrs[addr.Variable.Name] = ourVal\\n304: \\t\\t\\thclCtx.Variables[\\\"var\\\"] = cty.ObjectVal(attrs)\\n305: \\t\\t} else {\\n306: \\t\\t\\thclCtx.Variables[\\\"var\\\"] = cty.ObjectVal(map[string]cty.Value{\\n307: \\t\\t\\t\\taddr.Variable.Name: ourVal,\\n308: \\t\\t\\t})\\n309: \\t\\t}\\n310: \\t}\\n311: \\n312: \\tfor ix, validation := range rules {\\n313: \\t\\tresult, ruleDiags := evalVariableValidation(validation, hclCtx, valueRng, addr, ix, validateWalk)\\n314: \\t\\tdiags = diags.Append(ruleDiags)\\n315: \\n316: \\t\\tlog.Printf(\\\"[TRACE] evalVariableValidations: %s status is now %s\\\", addr, result.Status)\\n317: \\t\\tif result.Status == checks.StatusFail {\\n318: \\t\\t\\tcheckState.ReportCheckFailure(addr, addrs.InputValidation, ix, result.FailureMessage)\\n319: \\t\\t} else {\\n320: \\t\\t\\tcheckState.ReportCheckResult(addr, addrs.InputValidation, ix, result.Status)\\n321: \\t\\t}\\n322: \\t}\\n323: \\n324: \\treturn diags\\n325: }\\n326: \\n327: func evalVariableValidation(validation *configs.CheckRule, hclCtx *hcl.EvalContext, valueRng hcl.Range, addr addrs.AbsInputVariableInstance, ix int, validateWalk bool) (checkResult, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {\\n328: \\tconst errInvalidCondition = \\\"Invalid variable validation result\\\"\\n329: \\tconst errInvalidValue = \\\"Invalid value for variable\\\"\\n330: \\tvar diags tfdiags.Diagnostics\\n331: \\n332: \\tresult, moreDiags := validation.Condition.Value(hclCtx)\\n333: \\tdiags = diags.Append(moreDiags)\\n334: \\terrorValue, errorDiags := validation.ErrorMessage.Value(hclCtx)\\n335: \\n336: \\t// The following error handling is a workaround to preserve backwards\\n337: \\t// compatibility. Due to an implementation quirk, all prior versions of\\n338: \\t// Terraform would treat error messages specified using JSON\\n339: \\t// configuration syntax (.tf.json) as string literals, even if they\\n340: \\t// contained the \\\"${\\\" template expression operator. As we add support for error message expressions, we are\\n347: \\t// unable to perfectly distinguish between these two cases.\\n348: \\t//\\n349: \\t// To ensure that we don't break backwards compatibility, we have the\\n350: \\t// below fallback logic if the error message fails to evaluate. Passing\\n356: \\t\\t// nil as the context forces a JSON syntax string value to be\\n357: \\t\\t// interpreted as a string literal.\\n358: \\t\\tvar errorString string\\n359: \\t\\tmoreErrorDiags := gohcl.DecodeExpression(validation.ErrorMessage, nil, \\u0026errorString)\\n360: \\t\\tif !moreErrorDiags.HasErrors() {\\n361: \\t\\t\\t// Decoding succeeded, meaning that this is a JSON syntax\\n362: \\t\\t\\t// string value. We rewrap that as a cty value to allow later\\n363: \\t\\t\\t// decoding to succeed.\\n364: \\t\\t\\terrorValue = cty.StringVal(errorString)\\n365: \\n366: \\t\\t\\t// This warning diagnostic explains this odd behaviour, while\\n367: \\t\\t\\t// giving us an escape hatch to change this to a hard failure\\n368: \\t\\t\\t// in some future Terraform 1.x version.\\n369: \\t\\t\\terrorDiags = hcl.Diagnostics{\\n370: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\u0026hcl.Diagnostic{\\n371: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\tSeverity:    hcl.DiagWarning,\\n372: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\tSummary:     \\\"Validation error message expression is invalid\\\",\\n373: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\tDetail:      fmt.Sprintf(\\\"The error message provided could not be evaluated as an expression, so Terraform is interpreting it as a string literal.\\\\n\\\\nIn future versions of Terraform, this will be considered an error. Please file a GitHub issue if this would break your workflow.\\\\n\\\\n%s\\\", errorDiags.Error()),\\n374: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\tSubject:     validation.ErrorMessage.Range().Ptr(),\\n375: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\tContext:     validation.DeclRange.Ptr(),\\n376: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\tExpression:  validation.ErrorMessage,\\n377: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\tEvalContext: hclCtx,\\n378: \\t\\t\\t\\t},\\n379: \\t\\t\\t}\\n380: \\t\\t}\\n381: \\n382: \\t\\t// We want to either report the original diagnostics if the\\n383: \\t\\t// fallback failed, or the warning generated above if it succeeded.\\n384: \\t\\tdiags = diags.Append(errorDiags)\\n385: \\t}\\n386: \\n387: \\tif diags.HasErrors() {\\n388: \\t\\tlog.Printf(\\\"[TRACE] evalVariableValidations: %s rule %s check rule evaluation failed: _root_ module variables are set, which would\n81: \t\t\t// mask this error with a more specific one that refers to the\n82: \t\t\t// CLI features for setting such variables. We can get here for\n83: \t\t\t// child module variables, though.\n84: \t\t\tlog.Printf(\"[ERROR] prepareFinalInputVariableValue: %s is required but is not set\", addr)\n85: \t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{\n86: \t\t\t\tSeverity: hcl.DiagError,\n87: \t\t\t\tSummary:  `Required variable not set`,\n88: \t\t\t\tDetail:   fmt.Sprintf(`The variable %q is required, but is not set.`, addr.Variable.Name),\n89: \t\t\t\tSubject:  cfg.DeclRange.Ptr(),\n90: \t\t\t})\n91: \t\t\t// We'll return a placeholder unknown value to avoid producing\n92: \t\t\t// redundant downstream errors.\n93: \t\t\treturn cty.UnknownVal(cfg.Type), diags\n94: \t\t}\n95: \n96: \t\tgiven = defaultVal // must be set, because we checked above that the variable isn't required\n97: \t}\n98: \n99: \t// Apply defaults from the variable's type constraint to the converted value,\n100: \t// unless the converted value is null. We do not apply defaults to top-level\n101: \t// null values, as doing so could prevent assigning null to a nullable\n102: \t// variable.\n103: \tif cfg.TypeDefaults != nil && !given.IsNull() {\n104: \t\tgiven = cfg.TypeDefaults.Apply(given)\n105: \t}\n106: \n107: \tval, err := convert.Convert(given, convertTy)\n108: \tif err != nil {\n109: \t\tlog.Printf(\"[ERROR] prepareFinalInputVariableValue: %s has unsuitable type\\n  got:  %s\\n  want: %s\", addr, given.Type(), convertTy)\n110: \t\tvar detail string\n111: \t\tvar subject *hcl.Range\n112: \t\tif nonFileSource != \"\" {\n113: \t\t\tdetail = fmt.Sprintf(\n114: \t\t\t\t\"Unsuitable value for %s %s: %s.\",\n115: \t\t\t\taddr, nonFileSource, err,\n116: \t\t\t)\n117: \t\t\tsubject = cfg.DeclRange.Ptr()\n118: \t\t} else {\n119: \t\t\tdetail = fmt.Sprintf(\n120: \t\t\t\t\"The given value is not suitable for %s declared at %s: %s.\",\n121: \t\t\t\taddr, cfg.DeclRange.String(), err,\n122: \t\t\t)\n123: \t\t\tsubject = sourceRange.ToHCL().Ptr()\n124: \n125: \t\t\t// In some workflows, the operator running terraform does not have access to the variables\n126: \t\t\t// themselves. They are for example stored in encrypted files that will be used by the CI toolset\n127: \t\t\t// and not by the operator directly. In such a case, the failing secret value should not be\n128: \t\t\t// displayed to the operator\n129: \t\t\tif cfg.Sensitive {\n130: \t\t\t\tdetail = fmt.Sprintf(\n131: \t\t\t\t\t\"The given value is not suitable for %s, which is sensitive: %s. Invalid value defined at %s.\",\n132: \t\t\t\t\taddr, err, sourceRange.ToHCL(),\n133: \t\t\t\t)\n134: \t\t\t\tsubject = cfg.DeclRange.Ptr()\n135: \t\t\t}\n136: \t\t}\n137: \n138: \t\tdiags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{\n139: \t\t\tSeverity: hcl.DiagError,\n140: \t\t\tSummary:  \"Invalid value for input variable\",\n141: \t\t\tDetail:   detail,\n142: \t\t\tSubject:  subject,\n143: \t\t})\n144: \t\t// We'll return a placeholder unknown value to avoid producing\n145: \t\t// redundant downstream errors.\n146: \t\treturn cty.UnknownVal(cfg.Type), diags\n147: \t}\n148: \n149: \t// By the time we get here, we know:\n150: \t// - val matches the variable's type constraint\n151: \t// - val is definitely not cty.NilVal, but might be a null value if the given was already null.\n152: \t//\n153: \t// That means we just need to handle the case where the value is null,\n154: \t// which might mean we need to use the default value, or produce an error.\n155: \t//\n156: \t// For historical reasons we do this only for a \"non-nullable\" variable.\n157: \t// Nullable variables just appear as null if they were set to null,\n158: \t// regardless of any default value.\n159: \tif val.IsNull() && !cfg.Nullable {\n160: \t\tlog.Printf(\"[TRACE] prepareFinalInputVariableValue: %s is defined as null\", addr)\n161: \t\tif defaultVal != cty.NilVal {\n162: \t\t\tval = defaultVal\n163: \t\t} else {\n164: \t\t\tlog.Printf(\"[ERROR] prepareFinalInputVariableValue: %s is non-nullable but set to null, and is required\", addr)\n165: \t\t\tif nonFileSource != \"\" {\n166: \t\t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{\n167: \t\t\t\t\tSeverity: hcl.DiagError,\n168: \t\t\t\t\tSummary:  `Required variable not set`,\n169: \t\t\t\t\tDetail: fmt.Sprintf(\n170: \t\t\t\t\t\t\"Unsuitable value for %s %s: required variable may not be set to null.\",\n171: \t\t\t\t\t\taddr, nonFileSource,\n172: \t\t\t\t\t),\n173: \t\t\t\t\tSubject: cfg.DeclRange.Ptr(),\n174: \t\t\t\t})\n175: \t\t\t} else {\n176: \t\t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{\n177: \t\t\t\t\tSeverity: hcl.DiagError,\n178: \t\t\t\t\tSummary:  `Required variable not set`,\n179: \t\t\t\t\tDetail: fmt.Sprintf(\n180: \t\t\t\t\t\t\"The given value is not suitable for %s defined at %s: required variable may not be set to null.\",\n181: \t\t\t\t\t\taddr, cfg.DeclRange.String(),\n182: \t\t\t\t\t),\n183: \t\t\t\t\tSubject: sourceRange.ToHCL().Ptr(),\n184: \t\t\t\t})\n185: \t\t\t}\n186: \t\t\t// Stub out our return value so that the semantic checker doesn't\n187: \t\t\t// produce redundant downstream errors.\n188: \t\t\tval = cty.UnknownVal(cfg.Type)\n189: \t\t}\n190: \t}\n191: \n192: \tif cfg.Ephemeral {\n193: \t\t// An ephemeral input variable always has an ephemeral value inside the\n194: \t\t// module, even if the value assigned to it from outside is not. Changing the ephemerality of an\n198: \t\t// input variable is a breaking change to a module's API.\n199: \t\tval = val.Mark(marks.Ephemeral)\n200: \t} else {\n201: \t\tif marks.Contains(val, marks.Ephemeral) {\n202: \t\t\tvar subject hcl.Range\n203: \t\t\tif raw.HasSourceRange() {\n204: \t\t\t\tsubject = raw.SourceRange.ToHCL()\n205: \t\t\t} else {\n206: \t\t\t\t// We shouldn't typically get here for ephemeral values, because\n207: \t\t\t\t// all of the source types that can represent expressions that\n208: \t\t\t\t// could potentially produce ephemeral values are those which\n209: \t\t\t\t// have source locations. This is just here for robustness.\n210: \t\t\t\tsubject = cfg.DeclRange\n211: \t\t\t}\n212: \n213: \t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{\n214: \t\t\t\tSeverity: hcl.DiagError,\n215: \t\t\t\tSummary:  \"Ephemeral value not allowed\",\n216: \t\t\t\tDetail:   \"This input variable is not declared as accepting a ephemeral values, so it cannot be set to a result derived from an ephemeral value.\",\n217: \t\t\t\tSubject:  subject.Ptr(),\n218: \t\t\t})\n219: \t\t}\n220: \t}\n221: \n222: \treturn val, diags\n223: }\n224: \n225: // evalVariableValidations ensures that all of the configured custom validations\n226: // for a variable are passing.\n227: //\n228: // This must be used only after any side-effects that make the value of the\n229: // variable available for use in expression evaluation, such as\n230: // EvalModuleCallArgument for variables in descendant modules.\n231: func evalVariableValidations(addr addrs.AbsInputVariableInstance, ctx EvalContext, rules []*configs.CheckRule, valueRng hcl.Range, validateWalk bool) (diags tfdiags.Diagnostics) {\n232: \tif len(rules) == 0 {\n233: \t\tlog.Printf(\"[TRACE] evalVariableValidations: no validation rules declared for %s, so skipping\", addr)\n234: \t\treturn nil\n235: \t}\n236: \tlog.Printf(\"[TRACE] evalVariableValidations: validating %s\", addr)\n237: \n238: \tcheckState := ctx.Checks()\n239: \tif !checkState.ConfigHasChecks(addr.ConfigCheckable()) {\n240: \t\t// We have nothing to do if this object doesn't have any checks,\n241: \t\t// but the \"rules\" slice should agree that we don't.\n242: \t\tif ct := len(rules); ct != 0 {\n243: \t\t\tpanic(fmt.Sprintf(\"check state says that %s should have no rules, but it has %d\", addr, ct))\n244: \t\t}\n245: \t\treturn diags\n246: \t}\n247: \n248: \t// We'll build just one evaluation context covering the data needed by\n249: \t// all of the rules together, since that'll minimize lock contention\n250: \t// on the state, plan, etc.\n251: \tscope := ctx.EvaluationScope(nil, nil, EvalDataForNoInstanceKey)\n252: \tvar refs []*addrs.Reference\n253: \tfor _, rule := range rules {\n254: \t\tcondRefs, moreDiags := langrefs.ReferencesInExpr(addrs.ParseRef, rule.Condition)\n255: \t\tdiags = diags.Append(moreDiags)\n256: \t\tmsgRefs, moreDiags := langrefs.ReferencesInExpr(addrs.ParseRef, rule.ErrorMessage)\n257: \t\tdiags = diags.Append(moreDiags)\n258: \t\trefs = append(refs, condRefs...)\n259: \t\trefs = append(refs, msgRefs...)\n260: \t}\n261: \tif diags.HasErrors() {\n262: \t\t// If any of the references were invalid then evaluating the expressions\n263: \t\t// will duplicate those errors, so we'll bail out early.\n264: \t\treturn diags\n265: \t}\n266: \thclCtx, moreDiags := scope.EvalContext(refs)\n267: \tdiags = diags.Append(moreDiags)\n268: \tif moreDiags.HasErrors() {\n269: \t\treturn diags\n270: \t}\n271: \n272: \t// HACK: Historically we manually built a very constrained hcl.EvalContext\n273: \t// here, which only included the value of the one specific input variable\n274: \t// we're validating, since we didn't yet support referring to anything\n275: \t// else. That accidentally bypassed our rule that input variables are\n276: \t// always unknown during the validate walk, and thus accidentally created\n277: \t// a useful behavior of actually checking constant-only values against\n278: \t// their validation rules just during \"terraform validate\", rather than\n279: \t// having to run \"terraform plan\".\n280: \t//\n281: \t// Although that behavior was accidental, it makes simple validation rules\n282: \t// more useful and is protected by compatibility promises, and so we'll\n283: \t// fake it here by overwriting the unknown value that scope.EvalContext\n284: \t// will have inserted with a possibly-more-known value using the same\n285: \t// strategy our special code used to use.\n286: \tourVal := ctx.NamedValues().GetInputVariableValue(addr)\n287: \tif ourVal != cty.NilVal {\n288: \t\t// (it would be weird for ourVal to be nil here, but we'll tolerate it\n289: \t\t// because it was scope.EvalContext's responsibility to check for the\n290: \t\t// absent final value, and even if it didn't we'll just get an\n291: \t\t// evaluation error when evaluating the expressions below anyway.)\n292: \n293: \t\t// Our goal here is to make sure that a reference to the variable\n294: \t\t// we're checking will evaluate to ourVal, regardless of what else\n295: \t\t// scope.EvalContext might have put in the variables table.\n296: \t\tif hclCtx.Variables == nil {\n297: \t\t\thclCtx.Variables = make(map[string]cty.Value)\n298: \t\t}\n299: \t\tif varsVal, ok := hclCtx.Variables[\"var\"]; ok {\n300: \t\t\t// Unfortunately we need to unpack and repack the object here,\n301: \t\t\t// because cty values are immutable.\n302: \t\t\tattrs := varsVal.AsValueMap()\n303: \t\t\tattrs[addr.Variable.Name] = ourVal\n304: \t\t\thclCtx.Variables[\"var\"] = cty.ObjectVal(attrs)\n305: \t\t} else {\n306: \t\t\thclCtx.Variables[\"var\"] = cty.ObjectVal(map[string]cty.Value{\n307: \t\t\t\taddr.Variable.Name: ourVal,\n308: \t\t\t})\n309: \t\t}\n310: \t}\n311: \n312: \tfor ix, validation := range rules {\n313: \t\tresult, ruleDiags := evalVariableValidation(validation, hclCtx, valueRng, addr, ix, validateWalk)\n314: \t\tdiags = diags.Append(ruleDiags)\n315: \n316: \t\tlog.Printf(\"[TRACE] evalVariableValidations: %s status is now %s\", addr, result.Status)\n317: \t\tif result.Status == checks.StatusFail {\n318: \t\t\tcheckState.ReportCheckFailure(addr, addrs.InputValidation, ix, result.FailureMessage)\n319: \t\t} else {\n320: \t\t\tcheckState.ReportCheckResult(addr, addrs.InputValidation, ix, result.Status)\n321: \t\t}\n322: \t}\n323: \n324: \treturn diags\n325: }\n326: \n327: func evalVariableValidation(validation *configs.CheckRule, hclCtx *hcl.EvalContext, valueRng hcl.Range, addr addrs.AbsInputVariableInstance, ix int, validateWalk bool) (checkResult, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {\n328: \tconst errInvalidCondition = \"Invalid variable validation result\"\n329: \tconst errInvalidValue = \"Invalid value for variable\"\n330: \tvar diags tfdiags.Diagnostics\n331: \n332: \tresult, moreDiags := validation.Condition.Value(hclCtx)\n333: \tdiags = diags.Append(moreDiags)\n334: \terrorValue, errorDiags := validation.ErrorMessage.Value(hclCtx)\n335: \n336: \t// The following error handling is a workaround to preserve backwards\n337: \t// compatibility. Due to an implementation quirk, all prior versions of\n338: \t// Terraform would treat error messages specified using JSON\n339: \t// configuration syntax (.tf.json) as string literals, even if they\n340: \t// contained the \"${\" template expression operator. This behaviour did\n341: \t// not match that of HCL configuration syntax, where a template\n342: \t// expression would result in a validation error.\n343: \t//\n344: \t// As a result, users writing or generating JSON configuration syntax\n345: \t// may have specified error messages which are invalid template\n346: \t// expressions. As we add support for error message expressions, we are\n347: \t// unable to perfectly distinguish between these two cases.\n348: \t//\n349: \t// To ensure that we don't break backwards compatibility, we have the\n350: \t// below fallback logic if the error message fails to evaluate. This\n351: \t// should only have any effect for JSON configurations. The gohcl\n352: \t// DecodeExpression function behaves differently when the source of the\n353: \t// expression is a JSON configuration file and a nil context is passed.\n354: \tif errorDiags.HasErrors() {\n355: \t\t// Attempt to decode the expression as a string literal. Passing\n356: \t\t// nil as the context forces a JSON syntax string value to be\n357: \t\t// interpreted as a string literal.\n358: \t\tvar errorString string\n359: \t\tmoreErrorDiags := gohcl.DecodeExpression(validation.ErrorMessage, nil, &errorString)\n360: \t\tif !moreErrorDiags.HasErrors() {\n361: \t\t\t// Decoding succeeded, meaning that this is a JSON syntax\n362: \t\t\t// string value. We rewrap that as a cty value to allow later\n363: \t\t\t// decoding to succeed.\n364: \t\t\terrorValue = cty.StringVal(errorString)\n365: \n366: \t\t\t// This warning diagnostic explains this odd behaviour, while\n367: \t\t\t// giving us an escape hatch to change this to a hard failure\n368: \t\t\t// in some future Terraform 1.x version.\n369: \t\t\terrorDiags = hcl.Diagnostics{\n370: \t\t\t\t&hcl.Diagnostic{\n371: \t\t\t\t\tSeverity:    hcl.DiagWarning,\n372: \t\t\t\t\tSummary:     \"Validation error message expression is invalid\",\n373: \t\t\t\t\tDetail:      fmt.Sprintf(\"The error message provided could not be evaluated as an expression, so Terraform is interpreting it as a string literal.\\n\\nIn future versions of Terraform, this will be considered an error. Please file a GitHub issue if this would break your workflow.\\n\\n%s\", errorDiags.Error()),\n374: \t\t\t\t\tSubject:     validation.ErrorMessage.Range().Ptr(),\n375: \t\t\t\t\tContext:     validation.DeclRange.Ptr(),\n376: \t\t\t\t\tExpression:  validation.ErrorMessage,\n377: \t\t\t\t\tEvalContext: hclCtx,\n378: \t\t\t\t},\n379: \t\t\t}\n380: \t\t}\n381: \n382: \t\t// We want to either report the original diagnostics if the\n383: \t\t// fallback failed, or the warning generated above if it succeeded.\n384: \t\tdiags = diags.Append(errorDiags)\n385: \t}\n386: \n387: \tif diags.HasErrors() {\n388: \t\tlog.Printf(\"[TRACE] evalVariableValidations: %s rule %s check rule evaluation failed: %s\", addr, validation.DeclRange, diags.Err().Error())\n389: \t}\n390: \tif !result.IsKnown() {\n391: \t\tlog.Printf(\"[TRACE] evalVariableValidations: %s rule %s condition value is unknown, so skipping validation for now\", addr, validation.DeclRange)\n392: \n393: \t\treturn checkResult{Status: checks.StatusUnknown}, diags // We'll wait until we've learned more, then.\n394: \t}\n395: \tif result.IsNull() {\n396: \t\tdiags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{\n397: \t\t\tSeverity:    hcl.DiagError,\n398: \t\t\tSummary:     errInvalidCondition,\n399: \t\t\tDetail:      \"Validation condition expression must return either true or false, not null.\",\n400: \t\t\tSubject:     validation.Condition.Range().Ptr(),\n401: \t\t\tExpression:  validation.Condition,\n402: \t\t\tEvalContext: hclCtx,\n403: \t\t})\n404: \t\treturn checkResult{Status: checks.StatusError}, diags\n405: \t}\n406: \tvar err error\n407: \tresult, err = convert.Convert(result, cty.Bool)\n408: \tif err != nil {\n409: \t\tdiags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{\n410: tfdiags.FormatError(err)),\n413: \t\t\tSubject:     validation.Condition.Range().Ptr(),\n414: \t\t\tExpression:  validation.Condition,\n415: \t\t\tEvalContext: hclCtx,\n416: \t\t})\n417: \t\treturn checkResult{Status: checks.StatusError}, diags\n418: \t}\n419: \n420: \t// Validation condition may be marked if the input variable is bound to\n421: \t// a sensitive value. This is irrelevant to the validation process, so\n422: \t// we discard the marks now.\n423: \tresult, _ = result.Unmark()\n424: \tstatus := checks.StatusForCtyValue(result)\n425: \n426: \tif status != checks.StatusFail {\n427: \t\treturn checkResult{Status: status}, diags\n428: \t}\n429: \n430: \tif !errorValue.IsKnown() {\n431: \t\tif validateWalk {\n432: \t\t\tlog.Printf(\"[DEBUG] evalVariableValidations: %s rule %s error_message value is unknown, so skipping validation for now\", addr, validation.DeclRange)\n433: \t\t\treturn checkResult{Status: checks.StatusUnknown}, diags\n434: \t\t}\n435: \n436: \t\tdiags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{\n437: \t\t\tSeverity:    hcl.DiagError,\n438: \t\t\tSummary:     \"Invalid error message\",\n439: \t\t\tDetail:      \"Unsuitable value for error message: expression refers to values that won't be known until the apply phase.\",\n440: \t\t\tSubject:     validation.ErrorMessage.Range().Ptr(),\n441: \t\t\tExpression:  validation.ErrorMessage,\n442: \t\t\tEvalContext: hclCtx,\n443: \t\t\tExtra:       diagnosticCausedByUnknown(true),\n444: \t\t})\n445: \t\treturn checkResult{\n446: \t\t\tStatus: checks.StatusError,\n447: \t\t}, diags\n448: \t}\n449: \n450: \tvar errorMessage string\n451: \tif !errorDiags.HasErrors() && !errorValue.IsNull() {\n452: \t\tvar err error\n453: \t\terrorValue, err = convert.Convert(errorValue, cty.String)\n454: \t\tif err != nil {\n455: \t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{\n456: \t\t\t\tSeverity:    hcl.DiagError,\n457: \t\t\t\tSummary:     \"Invalid error message\",\n458: \t\t\t\tDetail:      fmt.Sprintf(\"Unsuitable value for error message: %s.\", tfdiags.FormatError(err)),\n459: \t\t\t\tSubject:     validation.ErrorMessage.Range().Ptr(),\n460: \t\t\t\tExpression:  validation.ErrorMessage,\n461: \t\t\t\tEvalContext: hclCtx,\n462: \t\t\t})\n463: \t\t} else {\n464: \t\t\tif marks.Has(errorValue, marks.Sensitive) {\n465: \t\t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{\n466: \t\t\t\t\tSeverity: hcl.DiagError,\n467: \n468: \t\t\t\t\tSummary: \"Error message refers to sensitive values\",\n469: \t\t\t\t\tDetail: `The error expression used to explain this condition refers to sensitive values. Terraform will not display the resulting message.\n470: \n471: You can correct this by removing references to sensitive values, or by carefully using the nonsensitive() function if the expression will not reveal the sensitive data.`,\n472: \n473: \t\t\t\t\tSubject:     validation.ErrorMessage.Range().Ptr(),\n474: \t\t\t\t\tExpression:  validation.ErrorMessage,\n475: \t\t\t\t\tEvalContext: hclCtx,\n476: \t\t\t\t})\n477: \t\t\t\terrorMessage = \"The error message included a sensitive value, so it will not be displayed.\"\n478: \t\t\t} else if marks.Has(errorValue, marks.Ephemeral) {\n479: \t\t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{\n480: \t\t\t\t\tSeverity: hcl.DiagError,\n481: \n482: \t\t\t\t\tSummary: \"Error message refers to ephemeral values\",\n483: \t\t\t\t\tDetail: `The error expression used to explain this condition refers to ephemeral values. Terraform will not display the resulting message.\n484: \n485: You can correct this by removing references to ephemeral values, or by carefully using the ephemeralasnull() function if the expression will not reveal the ephemeral data.`,\n486: \n487: \t\t\t\t\tSubject:     validation.ErrorMessage.Range().Ptr(),\n488: \t\t\t\t\tExpression:  validation.ErrorMessage,\n489: \t\t\t\t\tEvalContext: hclCtx,\n490: \t\t\t\t})\n491: \t\t\t\terrorMessage = \"The error message included a sensitive value, so it will not be displayed.\"\n492: \t\t\t} else {\n493: \t\t\t\terrorMessage = strings.TrimSpace(errorValue.AsString())\n494: \t\t\t}\n495: \t\t}\n496: \t}\n497: \tif errorMessage == \"\" {\n498: \t\terrorMessage = \"Failed to evaluate condition error message.\"\n499: \t}\n500: \n501: \tdiags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{\n502: \t\tSeverity:    hcl.DiagError,\n503: \t\tSummary:     errInvalidValue,\n504: \t\tDetail:      fmt.Sprintf(\"%s\\n\\nThis was checked by the validation rule at %s.\", errorMessage, validation.DeclRange.String()),\n505: \t\tSubject:     valueRng.Ptr(),\n506: \t\tExpression:  validation.Condition,\n507: \t\tEvalContext: hclCtx,\n508: \t\tExtra: &addrs.CheckRuleDiagnosticExtra{\n509: \t\t\tCheckRule: addr.CheckRule(addrs.InputValidation, ix),\n510: \t\t},\n511: \t})\n512: \n513: \treturn checkResult{\n514: \t\tStatus:         status,\n515: \t\tFailureMessage: errorMessage,\n516: \t}, diags\n517: }\n518: ", "url": "https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/terraform--v1.10.3@9802216fb24076bc614d9fa7961a9d85f6d7ec48/-/blob/internal/terraform/eval_variable.go"}, "isError": false}

## 2. `eval_variable.go`

Looking at `prepareFinalInputVariableValue()`:

Lines 192-220:
```go
if cfg.Ephemeral {
    // An ephemeral input variable always has an ephemeral value inside the
    // module, even if the value assigned to it from outside is not. This
    // is a useful simplification so that module authors can be explicit
    // about what guarantees they are intending to make (regardless of
    // current implementation details). Changing the ephemerality of an

    val = val.Mark(marks.Ephemeral)
```

This looks correct.

Wait, but there's something suspicious. Notice the function returns `val` at the end (line 222). But the Sensitive mark is not applied here! Let me re-read...

Looking at the function more carefully:
- `cfg.Ephemeral` → marks with `marks.Ephemeral` 
- But where is `marks.Sensitive` applied?

The function `prepareFinalInputVariableValue` doesn't seem to apply the `Sensitive` mark! This is different from `GetInputVariable` which does apply both. BUT looking at the code in `evaluate.go`, `GetInputVariable` applies marks AFTER getting the value from `NamedValues`:

```go
val := d.Evaluator.NamedValues.GetInputVariableValue(...)

// Mark if sensitive and/or ephemeral
if config.Sensitive {
    val = val.Mark(marks.Sensitive)
}
if config.Ephemeral {
    val = val.Mark(marks.Ephemeral)
}
```

So `evaluate.go` does apply `Sensitive` and `Ephemeral` marks when reading from `NamedValues`. And `prepareFinalInputVariableValue` handles the mark before storing into `NamedValues`. 

Wait, but `prepareFinalInputVariableValue` DOES handle ephemeral marks. But doesn't `Sensitive` get marked here too? Looking at the code... no, there's no `marks.Sensitive` being applied in `prepareFinalInputVariableValue`. 

Let me look at what happens with sensitive values. The sensitive mark is applied in `GetInputVariable` in `evaluate.go` when the value is retrieved. But in `prepareFinalInputVariableValue` in `eval_variable.go`, the sensitive mark is NOT applied. This is probably intentional because `evaluate.go` applies it.

Actually wait - the question is whether the value stored in NamedValues should or should not have the mark. Let me think about this differently... 

In `evaluate.go`'s `GetInputVariable`:
- Gets the value from `NamedValues` (unmarked)
- Applies `Sensitive` mark if `config.Sensitive`
- Applies `Ephemeral` mark if `config.Ephemeral`

In `eval_variable.go`'s `prepareFinalInputVariableValue`:
- Applies `Ephemeral` mark if `cfg.Ephemeral`
- Checks if value contains `Ephemeral` mark (for the else branch, to detect non-ephemeral vars being set to ephemeral values)

Hmm, so the value stored in `NamedValues` might already have the `Ephemeral` mark applied (from `prepareFinalInputVariableValue`), and then `GetInputVariable` would apply it AGAIN? That would result in double-marking...

Actually wait, let me look at how marks work in cty. When you call `val.Mark(marks.Ephemeral)` on a value that already has that mark, it should be a no-op or it might result in double marks... Actually in cty, marks are sets, so applying the same mark twice is idempotent.

But there's a potential issue: if `prepareFinalInputVariableValue` marks the value as `Ephemeral`, and then `GetInputVariable` marks it again as `Ephemeral`, that's double-marking. But since marks are sets, it should be fine.

The bigger concern: `prepareFinalInputVariableValue` applies `marks.Ephemeral` to the value BEFORE it gets stored in NamedValues. Then when `GetInputVariable` retrieves it from NamedValues, it would get a value that ALREADY has the Ephemeral mark. Then it tries to apply the mark AGAIN. This could be the issue - the value in NamedValues should NOT have the mark (the mark should only be applied at read time), OR NamedValues stores unmarked values.

Actually, let me re-read this more carefully. In `prepareFinalInputVariableValue`:
```go
if cfg.Ephemeral {
    val = val.Mark(marks.Ephemeral)
}
```

And later in `GetInputVariable`:
```go
val := d.Evaluator.NamedValues.GetInputVariableValue(...)
if config.Ephemeral {
    val = val.Mark(marks.Ephemeral)
}
```

So if `prepareFinalInputVariableValue` stores a marked value in NamedValues, then `GetInputVariable` would double-mark it.

Actually, I realize I need to understand better what NamedValues stores and what interface it uses. Let me check if NamedValues strips marks when storing, or expects unmarked values.

But actually - this might not be the PR-introduced bug. The PR might have changed something else. Let me focus on what looks anomalous vs what the description says should be defects.

## 3. `context_apply.go`

- Calls `checkApplyTimeVariables` (line 134)
- Iterates over `plan.Changes.Resources` for import hooks (lines 142-157)
- Builds and walks the apply graph

At line 257:
```go
if vv, exists := gotValues[name]; !exists || vv.Value == cty.NilVal || vv.Value.IsNull() {
```

This checks if the value exists AND is not nil AND not null. But `vv.Value.IsNull()` would panic if `vv.Value` is `cty.NilVal` (the zero value) because you can't call `IsNull()` on `cty.NilVal`! 

Wait, actually in Go, the short-circuit evaluation means `|| vv.Value == cty.NilVal || vv.Value.IsNull()` - the third condition only evaluates if the second is false. So if `vv.Value == cty.NilVal` is true, then `vv.Value.IsNull()` won't be called.

339: 	val := d.Evaluator.NamedValues.GetLocalValue(addr.Absolute(d.ModulePath))
340: 	return val, diags
341: }
342: 
343: func (d *evaluationStateData) GetModule(addr addrs.ModuleCall, rng tfdiags.SourceRange) (cty.Value, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {
344: 	var diags tfdiags.Diagnostics
345: 
346: 	// Output results live in the module that declares them, which is one of
347: 	// the child module instances of our current module path.
348: 	moduleAddr := d.ModulePath.Module().Child(addr.Name)
349: 	absAddr := addr.Absolute(d.ModulePath)
350: 
351: 	parentCfg := d.Evaluator.Config.DescendantForInstance(d.ModulePath)
352: 	callConfig, ok := parentCfg.Module.ModuleCalls[addr.Name]
353: 	if !ok {
354: 		diags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{
355: 			Severity: hcl.DiagError,
356: 			Summary:  `Reference to undeclared module`,
357: 			Detail:   fmt.Sprintf(`The configuration contains no %s.`, moduleAddr),
358: 			Subject:  rng.ToHCL().Ptr(),
359: 		})
360: 		return cty.DynamicVal, diags
361: 	}
362: 
363: 	// We'll consult the configuration to see what output names we are
364: 	// expecting, so we can ensure the resulting object is of the expected
365: 	// type even if our data is incomplete for some reason.
366: 	moduleConfig := d.Evaluator.Config.Descendant(moduleAddr)
367: 	if moduleConfig == nil {
368: 		// should never happen, since we have a valid module call above, this
369: 		// should be caught during static validation.
370: 		panic(fmt.Sprintf("output value read from %s, which has no configuration", moduleAddr))
371: 	}
372: 	outputConfigs := moduleConfig.Module.Outputs
373: 
374: 	// We don't do instance expansion during validation, and so we need to
375: 	// return an unknown value. Technically we should always return
376: 	// cty.DynamicVal here because the final value during plan will always
377: 	// be an object or tuple type with unpredictable attributes/elements,
378: 	// but because we never actually carry values forward from validation to
379: 	// planning we lie a little here and return unknown list and map types,
380: 	// just to give us more opportunities to catch author mistakes during
381: 	// validation.
382: 	//
383: 	// This means that in practice any expression that refers to a module
384: 	// call must be written to be valid for either a collection type or
385: 	// structural type of similar kind, so that it can be considered as
386: 	// valid during both the validate and plan walks.
387: 	if d.Operation == walkValidate {
388: 		atys := make(map[string]cty.Type, len(outputConfigs))
389: 		for name := range outputConfigs {
390: 			atys[name] = cty.DynamicPseudoType // output values are dynamically-typed
391: 		}
392: 		instTy := cty.Object(atys)
393: 
394: 		switch {
395: 		case callConfig.Count != nil:
396: 			return cty.UnknownVal(cty.List(instTy)), diags
397: 		case callConfig.ForEach != nil:
398: 			return cty.UnknownVal(cty.Map(instTy)), diags
399: 		default:
400: 			return cty.UnknownVal(instTy), diags
401: 		}
402: 	}
403: 
404: 	// For all other walk types, we proceed to dynamic evaluation of individual
405: 	// instances, using the global instance expander. An earlier graph node
406: 	// should always have registered the expansion of this module call before
407: 	// we get here, unless there's a bug in the graph builders.
408: 	allInstances := d.Evaluator.Instances
409: 	instKeyType, instKeys, known := allInstances.ExpandAbsModuleCall(absAddr)
410: 	if !known {
411: 		// If we don't know which instances exist then we can't really predict
412: 		// anything at all. We can't even predict the return type based on
413: 		// instKeyType because output values are dynamically-typed and so
414: 		// our final result will always be an object or tuple type whose
415: 		// attribute/element count we cannot predict.
416: 		return cty.DynamicVal, diags
417: 	}
418: 
419: 	instanceObjVal := func(instKey addrs.InstanceKey) (cty.Value, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {
420: 		// This function must always return a valid value, even if it's
421: 		// just a cty.DynamicVal placeholder accompanying error diagnostics.
422: 		var diags tfdiags.Diagnostics
423: 
424: 		namedVals := d.Evaluator.NamedValues
425: 		moduleInstAddr := absAddr.Instance(instKey)
426: 		attrs := make(map[string]cty.Value, len(outputConfigs))
427: 		for name := range outputConfigs {
428: 			outputAddr := moduleInstAddr.OutputValue(name)
429: 
430: 			// Although we do typically expect the graph dependencies to
431: 			// ensure that values get registered before they are needed,
432: 			// we track depedencies with specific output values where
433: 			// possible, instead of with entire module calls, and so
434: 			// in this specific case it's valid for some of this call's
435: 			// output values to not be known yet, with the graph builder
436: 			// being responsible for making sure that no expression
437: 			// in the configuration can actually observe that.
438: 			if !namedVals.HasOutputValue(outputAddr) {
439: 				attrs[name] = cty.DynamicVal
440: 				continue
441: 			}
442: 			outputVal := namedVals.GetOutputValue(outputAddr)
443: 			attrs[name] = outputVal
444: 		}
445: 
446: 		return cty.ObjectVal(attrs), diags
447: 	}
448: 
449: 	switch instKeyType {
450: 
451: 	case addrs.NoKeyType:
452: 		// In this case we should always have exactly one instance that
453: 		// is addrs.NoKey. If not then there's a bug in the [instances.Expander]
454: 		// implementation.
455: 		if len(instKeys) != 1 {
456: 			panic(fmt.Sprintf("module call has no instance key type but has %d instances (should be 1)", len(instKeys)))
457: 		}
458: 		ret, moreDiags := instanceObjVal(instKeys[0])
459: 		diags = diags.Append(moreDiags)
460: 		return ret, diags
461: 
462: 	case addrs.IntKeyType:
463: 		// We can assume that the instance keys are in ascending numerical order
464: 		// and are consecutive, per the contract of allInstances.ExpandModuleCall.
465: 		elems := make([]cty.Value, 0, len(instKeys))
466: 		for _, instKey := range instKeys {
467: 			instVal, moreDiags := instanceObjVal(instKey)
468: 			elems = append(elems, instVal)
469: 			diags = diags.Append(moreDiags)
470: 		}
471: 		return cty.TupleVal(elems), diags
472: 
473: 	case addrs.StringKeyType:
474: 		attrs := make(map[string]cty.Value, len(instKeys))
475: 		for _, instKey := range instKeys {
476: 			instVal, moreDiags := instanceObjVal(instKey)
477: 			attrs[string(instKey.(addrs.StringKey))] = instVal
478: 			diags = diags.Append(moreDiags)
479: 		}
480: 		return cty.ObjectVal(attrs), diags
481: 
482: 	default:
483: 		diags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{
484: 			Severity: hcl.DiagError,
485: 			Summary:  `Unsupported instance key type`,
486: 			Detail: fmt.Sprintf(
487: 				`Module call %s has instance key type %#v, which is not supported by the expression evaluator. This is a bug in Terraform.`,
488: 				absAddr, instKeyType,
489: 			),
490: 			Subject: rng.ToHCL().Ptr(),
491: 		})
492: 		return cty.DynamicVal, diags
493: 	}
494: }
495: 
496: func (d *evaluationStateData) GetResource(addr addrs.Resource, rng tfdiags.SourceRange) (cty.Value, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {
497: 	var diags tfdiags.Diagnostics
498: 	// First we'll consult the configuration to see if an resource of this
499: 	// name is declared at all.
500: 	moduleAddr := d.ModulePath
501: 	moduleConfig := d.Evaluator.Config.DescendantForInstance(moduleAddr)
502: 	if moduleConfig == nil {
503: 		// should never happen, since we can't be evaluating in a module
504: 		// that wasn't mentioned in configuration.
505: 		panic(fmt.Sprintf("resource value read from %s, which has no configuration", moduleAddr))
506: 	}
507: 
508: 	config := moduleConfig.Module.ResourceByAddr(addr)
509: 	if config == nil {
510: 		diags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{
511: 			Severity: hcl.DiagError,
512: 			Summary:  `Reference to undeclared resource`,
513: 			Detail:   fmt.Sprintf(`A resource %q %q has not been declared in %s`, addr.Type, addr.Name, moduleDisplayAddr(moduleAddr)),
514: 			Subject:  rng.ToHCL().Ptr(),
515: 		})
516: 		return cty.DynamicVal, diags
517: 	}
518: 
519: 	// Much of this function was written before we had factored out the handling
520: 	// of instance keys into the separate instance expander model, and so it
521: 	// does a bunch of instance-related work itself below.
522: 	//
523: 	// Currently, unknown instance keys are only possible when planning with
524: 	// DeferralAllowed set to true in the PlanOpts, which should only be the
525: 	// case in the stacks runtime (not the "normal terraform" modules runtime).
526: 	// Thus, we have some amount of duplicated code remaining, to be more
527: 	// certain that stacks-specific behaviors won't leak out into the standard
528: 	// runtime.
529: 	//
530: 	// TODO: When deferred actions are more stable and robust in stacks, it
531: 	// would be nice to rework this function to rely on the ResourceInstanceKeys
532: 	// result for _all_ of its work, rather than continuing to duplicate a bunch
533: 	// of the logic we've tried to encapsulate over ther already.
534: 	if d.Operation == walkPlan || d.Operation == walkApply {
535: 		if _, _, hasUnknownKeys := d.Evaluator.Instances.ResourceInstanceKeys(addr.Absolute(moduleAddr)); hasUnknownKeys {
536: 			// There really isn't anything interesting we can do in this situation,
537: 			// because it means we have an unknown for_each/count, in which case
538: 			// we can't even predict what the result type will be because it
539: 			// would be either an object or tuple type decided based on the instance
540: 			// keys.
541: 			// (We can't get in here for a single-instance resource because in that
542: 			// case we would know that there's only one key and it's addrs.NoKey,
543: 			// so we'll fall through to the other logic below.)
544: 			unknownVal := cty.DynamicVal
545: 
546: 			// If an ephemeral resource is deferred we need to mark the returned unknown value as ephemeral
547: 			if addr.Mode == addrs.EphemeralResourceMode {
548: 				unknownVal = unknownVal.Mark(marks.Ephemeral)
549: 			}
550: 			return unknownVal, diags
551: 		}
552: 	}
553: 
554: 	// Build the provider address from configuration, since we may not have
555: 	// state available in all cases.
556: 	// We need to build an abs provider address, but we can use a default
557: 	// instance since we're only interested in the schema.
558: 	schema := d.getResourceSchema(addr, config.Provider)
559: 	if schema == nil {
560: 		// This shouldn't happen, since validation before we get here should've
561: 		// taken care of it, but we'll show a reasonable error message anyway.
562: 		diags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{
563: 			Severity: hcl.DiagError,
564: 			Summary:  `Missing resource type schema`,
565: 			Detail:   fmt.Sprintf("No schema is available for %s in %s. This is a bug in Terraform and should be reported.", addr, config.Provider),
566: 			Subject:  rng.ToHCL().Ptr(),
567: 		})
568: 		return cty.DynamicVal, diags
569: 	}
570: 	ty := schema.ImpliedType()
571: 
572: 	if addr.Mode == addrs.EphemeralResourceMode {
573: 		// FIXME: This does not yet work with deferrals, and it would be nice to
574: 		// find some way to refactor this so that the following code is not so
575: 		// tethered to the current implementation details. Instead we should
576: 		// have an abstract idea of first determining what instances the
577: 		// resource has (using d.Evaluator.Instances.ResourceInstanceKeys) and
578: 		// then retrieving the value for each instance to assemble into the
579: 		// result, using some per-resource-mode logic maintained elsewhere.
580: 		return d.getEphemeralResource(addr, rng)
581: 	}
582: 
583: 	rs := d.Evaluator.State.Resource(addr.Absolute(d.ModulePath))
584: 
585: 	if rs == nil {
586: 		switch d.Operation {
587: 		case walkPlan, walkApply:
588: 			// During plan and apply as we evaluate each removed instance they
589: 			// are removed from the working state. Since we know there are no
590: 			// instances, return an empty container of the expected type.
591: 			switch {
592: 			case config.Count != nil:
593: 				return cty.EmptyTupleVal, diags
594: 			case config.ForEach != nil:
595: 				return cty.EmptyObjectVal, diags
596: 			default:
597: 				// While we can reference an expanded resource with 0
598: 				// instances, we cannot reference instances that do not exist.
599: 				// Due to the fact that we may have direct references to
600: 				// instances that may end up in a root output during destroy
601: 				// (since a planned destroy cannot yet remove root outputs), we
602: 				// need to return a dynamic value here to allow evaluation to
603: 				// continue.
604: 				log.Printf("[ERROR] unknown instance %q referenced during %s", addr.Absolute(d.ModulePath), d.Operation)
605: 				return cty.DynamicVal, diags
606: 			}
607: 
608: 		case walkImport:
609: 			// Import does not yet plan resource changes, so new resources from
610: 			// config are not going to be found here. Once walkImport fully
611: 			// plans resources, this case should not longer be needed.
612: 			// In the single instance case, we can return a typed unknown value
613: 			// for the instance to better satisfy other expressions using the
614: 			// value. This of course will not help if statically known
615: 			// attributes are expected to be known elsewhere, but reduces the
616: 			// number of problematic configs for now.
617: 			// Unlike in plan and apply above we can't be sure the count or
618: 			// for_each instances are empty, so we return a DynamicVal. We
619: 			// don't really have a good value to return otherwise -- empty
620: 			// values will fail for direct index expressions, and unknown
621: 			// Lists and Maps could fail in some type unifications.
622: 			switch {
623: 			case config.Count != nil:
624: 				return cty.DynamicVal, diags
625: 			case config.ForEach != nil:
626: 				return cty.DynamicVal, diags
627: 			default:
628: 				return cty.UnknownVal(ty), diags
629: 			}
630: 
631: 		default:
632: 			// We should only end up here during the validate walk (or
633: 			// console/eval), since later walks should have at least partial
634: 			// states populated for all resources in the configuration.
635: 			return cty.DynamicVal, diags
636: 		}
637: 	}
638: 
639: 	// Now, we're going to build up a value that represents the resource
640: 	// or resources that are in the state.

Summary: USER_CONTEXT: Code review of a Terraform PR modifying plan/apply evaluation pipeline. Find defects (functional bugs, cross-file interaction bugs, data integrity bugs) in 4 files and produce `/workspace/review.json` with structured defect reports and unified diff fixes. Do NOT edit source files directly.

TASK_TRACKING:
- Task: Review 4 files for defects, write `/workspace/review.json`
- Status: IN PROGRESS — files read, analysis underway

COMPLETED:
- Read all 4 target files via MCP:
  - `internal/terraform/evaluate.go` (978 lines)
  - `internal/terraform/eval_variable.go` (518 lines)
  - `internal/terraform/context_apply.go` (~401 lines)
  - `internal/terraform/hook.go` (219 lines)

PENDING:
- Re-read truncated sections of `evaluate.go` (lines ~307–640, covering `GetLocalValue`, `GetModule`, `GetResource`)
- Complete defect analysis across all 4 files
- Write `/workspace/review.json`

CURRENT_STATE:
**Potential defects identified so far (preliminary):**

1. **`context_apply.go` ~lines 150-155** — Import hook dispatch ignores return values of `PreApplyImport`/`PostApplyImport` (returns `HookAction, error` but discarded). If a hook returns `HookActionHalt` or an error, it is silently ignored.

2. **`evaluate.go` GetResource (lines ~500-640)** — Truncated; deferred resource handling with unknown instance keys needs closer inspection. Need to re-read.

3. **`eval_variable.go` `prepareFinalInputVariableValue`** — Sensitive mark not applied here; mark is applied later in `evaluate.go`'s `GetInputVariable`. Need to verify this split is intentional/correct.

4. **`hook.go`** — `HookActionContinue=0` (iota), `HookActionHalt=1`. NilHook methods all return `HookActionContinue`. No obvious bug here yet.

CODE_STATE:
- `evaluate.go`: `GetInputVariable()` ~line 234, `GetResource()` ~line 500, `GetModule()` somewhere in 307-500
- `eval_variable.go`: `prepareFinalInputVariableValue()` line 24, ephemeral mark applied at ~line 192
- `context_apply.go`: `ApplyAndEval()` line 87, `checkApplyTimeVariables()` referenced, import hook dispatch ~line 150, `applyGraph()` ~line 270
- `hook.go`: `HookActionContinue=0`, `HookActionHalt=1`, `NilHook` all return Continue

DEPS:
- `marks.Sensitive`, `marks.Ephemeral` from `internal/lang/marks`
- `deferring.Deferred` for deferred resource tracking
- `namedvals.State` for variable values
